planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring after last frost in any well-drained soil; this is one of the few true shade-tolerant flowering shrubs that performs in deep shade while also thriving in full sun. Dies to the ground in hard frost zones but re-sprouts vigorously.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil in full sun to deep shade; Turk's cap is one of the South's most versatile and valuable flowering shrubs, producing bright red nodding flowers with tightly spiraled petals that never fully open (resembling a Turkish fez) from summer through frost — and in zone 9–10, flowering nearly year round. It is outstanding in dry shade under large trees where little else will bloom, and is equally at home in full blazing sun. Ruby-throated and rufous hummingbirds are its primary pollinators and mob the flowers from summer through migration. In zone 8 it dies back to the roots in hard freezes but re-sprouts reliably in spring and reaches full size by July. Do not overwater; it tolerates considerable drought once established.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; excellent as a specimen, screen, or hedge under tree canopy.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; Florida anise tree is one of the finest native evergreen shrubs for deep shade in the Deep South, producing lustrous dark green aromatic foliage (strongly anise-scented when crushed) that forms a dense, refined screen under mature trees. The unusual maroon-red star-shaped flowers in spring are striking at close range though small. It grows naturally in moist, shaded ravine heads and stream banks in the Florida Panhandle and adjacent states. An excellent substitute for the invasive Japanese anise tree. All parts of the plant are toxic; it is not related to culinary anise despite the scent. Performs best with consistent moisture and mulch to keep roots cool.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in late summer or fall; do not bury too deeply as shallow planting promotes better bloom. The bulbs have an unusual growth cycle — never remove foliage as it builds the bulb's energy reserve for next year's bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, moist to average soil in partial shade to full sun; red spider lily (originally from China and Japan, long naturalized in the American South) is famous for its 'surprise' appearance — naked stalks push up abruptly from bare ground in September with no foliage, opening into clusters of brilliant crimson flowers with long arching stamens, then disappearing as quickly, followed weeks later by winter-to-spring foliage that dies back in summer. Plant along path edges, under deciduous trees, or in massing where the unusual bloom timing creates a dramatic seasonal event. Do not disturb established colonies — they bloom best when crowded. The bulbs are toxic; plant away from areas where children might dig. A beloved heirloom bulb of Southern gardens.

planting.note: Plant at least one male and three to five female shrubs for berry production; match male and female cultivar bloom times. The male plant 'Red Escort' is widely used with female cultivars of this species.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; possum haw is a native deciduous holly of the Southeast naturally found in stream margins, swamp edges, and moist bottomland forests, valued for the brilliant orange-red berries that adorn bare branches all winter long after leaves drop in fall. It is an excellent alternative to winterberry (I. verticillata) for zone 8–9 gardens where winterberry can struggle with heat. Like all hollies, it is dioecious — both male and female plants are required for berry set, in a ratio of at least one male per five females. Cedar waxwings, robins, bluebirds, and mockingbirds rely on the persistent berries throughout winter. Named female cultivars like 'Warren's Red' and 'Fineline' are widely available.

planting.note: Plant large bulbs with the neck just at or above soil level in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade; does not tolerate dry conditions. Excellent for pond margins and rain gardens.

directions: Plant large bulbs with the neck at or just above soil level in consistently wet to moist, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; swamp lily is a stunning native bulb of the Gulf Coast, Florida, and Caribbean, growing naturally in freshwater swamps, bog margins, stream banks, and wet ditches. It produces large, fragrant, spider-like white flowers with narrow strap petals in summer and into fall — often repeatedly after summer rain events — on upright stalks among bold, strap-like evergreen leaves. Plant at pond margins, in consistently moist rain garden hollows, or in large containers kept in saucers of water on the patio. The bold foliage and exotic flowers provide a tropical look in Gulf Coast zone 8–9 gardens. Divide crowded clumps every 4–5 years by removing and replanting offsets.
